perturbation.original_text: 5. Custom er and Minnesota Power hereby acknowledge that unauthorized disclosure or use of the Inform ation m ay result in irreparable harm to Minnesota Power. Accordingly, Custom er agrees that Minnesota Power shall ha ve the right to obtain a tem porary restraining order enjoining disclosure or use of all Inform ation subject to this Agreem ent. Custom er agrees not to contest the granting of such tem porary restraining order. Such order shall becom e perm anent upon adequate showing of irreparable inju ry and ruling by a court of law. The Parties may participate in any contest relating to a perm anent injunction.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Custom er and Minnesota Power hereby acknowledge that unauthorized disclosure or use of the Inform ation m ay result in irreparable harm to Minnesota Power. Accordingly, Custom er agrees that Minnesota Power shall ha ve the right to obtain a tem porary restraining order enjoining disclosure or use of all Inform ation subject to this Agreem ent. Custom er agrees not to contest the granting of such tem porary restraining order. Such order shall becom e perm anent without any showing of irreparable injury or ruling by a court of law. The Parties may participate in any contest relating to a perm anent injunction.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for 'adequate showing of irreparable injury and ruling by a court of law' before a temporary restraining order becomes permanent is a legal contradiction because it circumvents the standard legal process for obtaining a permanent injunction.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 65 outlines the requirements for issuing preliminary injunctions and temporary restraining orders, which necessitate a showing of irreparable harm and a likelihood of success on the merits. Removing the 'showing of irreparable injury' contradicts these requirements, making the process for obtaining a permanent injunction inconsistent with federal law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders (a) Preliminary Injunction. (1) Notice. The court may issue a preliminary injunction only on notice to the adverse party. (2) Consolidating the Hearing with the Trial on the Merits. Before or after beginning the hearing on a motion for a preliminary injunction, the court may advance the trial on the merits and consolidate it with the hearing. Even when consolidation is not ordered, evidence that is received on the motion and that would be admissible at trial becomes part of the trial record and need not be repeated at trial. But the court must preserve any party's right to a jury trial. (b) Temporary Restraining Order. (1) Issuing Without Notice. The court may issue a temporary restraining order without written or oral notice to the adverse party or its attorney only if: (A) specific facts in an affidavit or a verified complaint clearly show that immediate and irreparable injury, loss, or damage will result to the movant before the adverse party can be heard in opposition; and (B) the movant's attorney certifies in writing any efforts made to give notice and the reasons why it should not be required. (2) Contents; Expiration. Every temporary restraining order issued without notice must state the date and hour it was issued; describe the injury and state why it is irreparable; state why the order was issued without notice; and be promptly filed in the clerk's office and entered in the record. The order expires at the time after entry—not to exceed 14 days—that the court sets, unless before that time the court, for good cause, extends it for a like period or the adverse party consents to a longer extension. The reasons for an extension must be entered in the record. (3) Expediting the Preliminary-Injunction Hearing. If the order is issued without notice, the moti
perturbation.justification: The modified contract eliminates the requirement for 'adequate showing of irreparable injury and ruling by a court of law' before a temporary restraining order becomes permanent. This directly contradicts FRCP 65, which, as reflected in Scraped Law Snippet 1, necessitates a showing of irreparable harm for issuing preliminary injunctions and temporary restraining orders. By removing this requirement, the changed text allows for a permanent injunction without meeting the legal threshold, creating a clear contradiction. The contract now allows for a permanent injunction without the necessary legal justification, violating the standard legal process.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Upon the written request of Minnesota Power, the Custom er shall return to Minnesota Power all docum entation and electr onic m edia containing Inform ation and shall provide written certif ication that copies of all Inf ormation have been destroyed or returned.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Upon the oral request of Minnesota Power, the Custom er shall immediately return to Minnesota Power all docum entation and electr onic m edia containing Inform ation and shall provide verbal certif ication that copies of all Inf ormation have been destroyed or returned.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the requirement from a 'written request' to an 'oral request' and 'written certification' to 'verbal certification' along with 'immediately return' creates ambiguity and contradicts the Statute of Frauds, particularly if the agreement involves substantial value or a long-term obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Minnesota Statute of Frauds, Minn. Stat. § 513.01
perturbation.law_explanation: The Minnesota Statute of Frauds requires certain agreements, particularly those involving significant consideration or long durations, to be in writing to be enforceable.  By removing the writing requirement for the request to return confidential information and the certification of destruction, the modified text potentially violates the statute of frauds, making the obligation unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 513.01 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes PROPERTY AND PROPERTY INTERESTS Chapter 513 Section 513.01 513.02 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 513 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 513.01 Version List 513.01 NO ACTION ON AGREEMENT. No action shall be maintained, in either of the following cases, upon any agreement, unless such agreement, or some note or memorandum thereof, expressing the consideration, is in writing, and subscribed by the party charged therewith: (1) every agreement that by its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof; (2) every special promise to answer for the debt, default or doings of another; (3) every agreement, promise, or undertaking made upon consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; (4) every agreement, promise or undertaking to pay a debt which has been discharged by bankruptcy or insolvency proceedings. History: ( 8456 ) RL s 3483 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the requirement for a request and certification from written to oral/verbal, potentially violating the Statute of Frauds. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, "No action shall be maintained... upon any agreement, unless such agreement, or some note or memorandum thereof, expressing the consideration, is in writing, and subscribed by the party charged therewith... (1) every agreement that by its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof." While the specific agreement's duration isn't provided, the change introduces a risk of unenforceability if the agreement falls under the Statute of Frauds, as the oral request and verbal certification lack the written form required for certain agreements.

perturbation.original_text: 9. This Agreement shall be effective for a term of ______ (__) years from the day first written above. Notwithstanding the Parties entering into any further agreement or arrangement, or any other event or occurrence, the provisions of this Agreement relating to nondisclosure (including the provisions relating to the breach of this Agreement) or use of the Information shall remain in full force and eff ect for ________ years or as long as the Information is confidential and non-public, whichever is less, unless otherwise agreed to in writing by the Parties hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. This Agreement shall be effective for a term of ______ (__) years from the day first written above. Notwithstanding the Parties entering into any further agreement or arrangement, or any other event or occurrence, the provisions of this Agreement relating to nondisclosure (including the provisions relating to the breach of this Agreement) or use of the Information shall remain in full force and eff ect indefinitely, even if the information becomes publicly available.
perturbation.explanation: Stating that the nondisclosure provisions remain in effect 'indefinitely, even if the information becomes publicly available' contradicts the general principles of trade secret law, which typically requires that the information remain confidential to be protected.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), which many states, including Minnesota, have adopted in some form, defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable by proper means. Making the confidentiality obligations indefinite, even if the information becomes public, goes against the core principle of trade secret law which requires that the information remain confidential to be protected under the UTSA. Minnesota adopted the UTSA in Minn. Stat. § 325C.01.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The changed text states that the nondisclosure provisions remain in effect 'indefinitely, even if the information becomes publicly available.' This directly contradicts the core principle of trade secret law, as highlighted by the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation, which requires that the information remain confidential to be protected. The Law Explanation references the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) and Minnesota's adoption of the UTSA in Minn. Stat. § 325C.01. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the Law Explanation states the change goes against the core principle of trade secret law, which requires that the information remain confidential to be protected under the UTSA.

perturbation.original_text: 11. Nothing contained in the Agreement sh all be construed as granting any rights under any patent, trademark or copyright, by licen se or otherwise, protecting any Information subject to this Agreement, and that this Agreem ent does not create a partnership, joint venture or other legal relationship between the Parties. The construction, interpretation and performance of this Agreement, and all transactions under it, shall be governed by the laws of the State of Minnesota, without regard to Minnesota law governi ng conflict of laws, even if one or more of the Parties hereto may be a resident of, or domiciled in, any other state or country.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. Nothing contained in the Agreement sh all be construed as granting any rights under any patent, trademark or copyright, by licen se or otherwise, protecting any Information subject to this Agreement, and that this Agreem ent does not create a partnership, joint venture or other legal relationship between the Parties. The construction, interpretation and performance of this Agreement, and all transactions under it, shall be governed by the laws of the State of North Dakota, regardless of Minnesota law governing conflict of laws, even if one or more of the Parties hereto may be a resident of, or domiciled in, any other state or country.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Minnesota to North Dakota could conflict with Minnesota's choice-of-law rules if the contract has a substantial connection to Minnesota, and the application of North Dakota law would violate a fundamental policy of Minnesota. Minnesota has a significant relationship to the transaction, and there is no compelling reason for North Dakota law to apply.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 187 of the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws allows parties to choose the governing law, but this choice is not absolute. A contractual choice of law provision will not be followed if the chosen state has no substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction and there is no other reasonable basis for the parties' choice, or application of the law of the chosen state would be contrary to a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state in the determination of the particular issue and which would be the state of the applicable law in the absence of an effective choice of law by the parties. Since the contract is between Minnesota Power and a customer, changing to North Dakota law could violate this if Minnesota has a materially greater interest.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies Minnesota law, while the changed text specifies North Dakota law. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state that changing the governing law from Minnesota to North Dakota could conflict with the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws, specifically Section 187. Since the scraped law snippets are not valid, the contradiction is based on the Law Explanation referring to a conflict of laws if Minnesota has a materially greater interest. This indicates that the modification introduces a potential legal contradiction by violating choice-of-law principles.
